initial_claims.description: The withdraw function sends Ether to msg.sender using low-level call before reducing the caller's recorded balance. Because .call forwards gas and executes attacker-controlled code, a malicious contract can re-enter withdraw() repeatedly and receive multiple Ether payouts while balances[msg.sender] still satisfies the initial check.
initial_claims.evidence: Code path: if (balances[msg.sender] >= _amount) { if (msg.sender.call.value(_amount)()) { _amount; } balances[msg.sender] -= _amount; }. The external interaction msg.sender.call.value(_amount)() occurs before the effect balances[msg.sender] -= _amount. Exploit trace: (1) attacker obtains a positive token balance, (2) attacker calls withdraw(_amount) from a contract, (3) fallback function re-enters withdraw(_amount) during the low-level call, (4) each nested call passes the unchanged balances[msg.sender] check and transfers Ether again, (5) balance is only decremented after unwinding, enabling Ether drain up to the contract's Ether balance.

claim.description: The withdraw function sends Ether to msg.sender using low-level call before reducing the caller's recorded balance. Because .call forwards gas and executes attacker-controlled code, a malicious contract can re-enter withdraw() repeatedly and receive multiple Ether payouts while balances[msg.sender] still satisfies the initial check.

The external interaction msg.sender.call.value(_amount)() occurs before the effect balances[msg.sender] -= _amount. Exploit trace: (1) attacker obtains a positive token balance, (2) attacker calls withdraw(_amount) from a contract, (3) fallback function re-enters withdraw(_amount) during the low-level call, (4) each nested call passes the unchanged balances[msg.sender] check and transfers Ether again, (5) balance is only decremented after unwinding, enabling Ether drain up to the contract's Ether balance.

verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern exists in Asiandragon.withdraw(uint _amount). The function checks `if(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount)`, then performs `msg.sender.call.value(_amount)()` before updating storage with `balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;`. That is a classic checks-effects-interactions violation on a full-gas external call. There is no `nonReentrant` modifier on `withdraw`, and the `ReentrancyGuard` contract defined earlier is not inherited by `Asiandragon`, so it provides no protection on this path. The exploit path is realistic: an attacker can first obtain token balance via normal ERC20 transfer, then call `withdraw(_amount)` from a contract account whose fallback re-enters `withdraw(_amount)` during the low-level call. Because the balance is not reduced until after each external call returns, each nested invocation sees the same pre-call `balances[msg.sender]` and can trigger another Ether transfer, draining the contract's Ether balance subject to available funds. The attacker description is directionally correct, though one nuance is that the final subtraction occurs once per stack frame, so under Solidity 0.4.25/SafeMath-less direct `-=` this can also underflow after enough reentries, further corrupting the recorded token balance. Impact is real and affects others if the contract holds Ether from third parties or accidental deposits: the attacker can extract Ether beyond what a single withdrawal should allow. The defender's argument is code-backed and correctly notes the absence of any applicable mitigation.
verdict.recommendation: Apply checks-effects-interactions: move `balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;` before any external call, and revert on failed payment. Prefer a pull-payment pattern or add a real reentrancy guard by inheriting `ReentrancyGuard` and marking `withdraw` as `nonReentrant`. Example: `require(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount); balances[msg.sender] = balances[msg.sender].sub(_amount); (bool ok,) = msg.sender.call.value(_amount)(""); require(ok);`. Also consider separating token balances from Ether accounting entirely, since this function currently lets token balances control Ether withdrawals.

The function checks `if(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount)`, then performs `msg.sender.call.value(_amount)()` before updating storage with `balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;`. That is a classic checks-effects-interactions violation on a full-gas external call. There is no `nonReentrant` modifier on `withdraw`, and the `ReentrancyGuard` contract defined earlier is not inherited by `Asiandragon`, so it provides no protection on this path. The exploit path is realistic: an attacker can first obtain token balance via normal ERC20 transfer, then call `withdraw(_amount)` from a contract account whose fallback re-enters `withdraw(_amount)` during the low-level call. Because the balance is not reduced until after each external call returns, each nested invocation sees the same pre-call `balances[msg.sender]` and can trigger another Ether transfer, draining the contract's Ether balance subject to available funds. The attacker description is directionally correct, though one nuance is that the final subtraction occurs once per stack frame, so under Solidity 0.4.25/SafeMath-less direct `-=` this can also underflow after enough reentries, further corrupting the recorded token balance. Impact is real and affects others if the contract holds Ether from third parties or accidental deposits: the attacker can extract Ether beyond what a single withdrawal should allow. The defender's argument is code-backed and correctly notes the absence of any applicable mitigation.

The cited reentrancy pattern is actually present in Asiandragon.withdraw(uint _amount). The function performs an external call to msg.sender using low-level call.value(_amount)() before updating storage with balances[msg.sender] -= _amount. There is no nonReentrant modifier applied to withdraw, no mutex check inside withdraw, and no CEI ordering. Solidity 0.4.25 provides no implicit reentrancy protection. Because balances[msg.sender] is checked before the external call and only decremented afterward, a malicious contract can re-enter withdraw() while its recorded balance is still unchanged.

The vulnerable pattern exists in Asiandragon.withdraw(uint _amount). The function checks `if(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount)`, then performs `msg.sender.call.value(_amount)()` before updating storage with `balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;`. That is a classic checks-effects-interactions violation on a full-gas external call. There is no `nonReentrant` modifier on `withdraw`, and the `ReentrancyGuard` contract defined earlier is not inherited by `Asiandragon`, so it provides no protection on this path. The exploit path is realistic: an attacker can first obtain token balance via normal ERC20 transfer, then call `withdraw(_amount)` from a contract account whose fallback re-enters `withdraw(_amount)` during the low-level call. Because the balance is not reduced until after each external call returns, each nested invocation sees the same pre-call `balances[msg.sender]` and can trigger another Ether transfer, draining the contract's Ether balance subject to available funds. The attacker description is directionally correct, though one nuance is that the final subtraction occurs once per stack frame, so under Solidity 0.4.25/SafeMath-less direct `-=` this can also underflow after enough reentries, further corrupting the recorded token balance. Impact is real and affects others if the contract holds Ether from third parties or accidental deposits: the attacker can extract Ether beyond what a single withdrawal should allow. The defender's argument is code-backed and correctly notes the absence of any applicable mitigation.
